perturbation.original_text: “ Confidential Information ” means any information, technical data, trade secrets or know-how of the disclosing party or its customers, vendors, business partners or investors that is provided to the recipient party by or on behalf of the disclosing party, either directly or indirectly, whether in writing, orally or by observation, including, but not limited to, research, products, services, product plans, clients, client lists, lead lists, markets, marketing, expansion plans, databases, software, developments, inventions, processes, technology, maskworks, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, finances, financial results or other business information, in each case which the disclosing party considers to be confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: “ Confidential Information ” means any information, technical data, or know-how of the disclosing party or its customers that is provided to the recipient party by or on behalf of the disclosing party, whether in writing, orally or by observation, including, but not limited to, research, products, services, product plans, clients, client lists, lead lists, markets, databases, software, developments, inventions, processes, technology, maskworks, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, finances, financial results or other business information, in each case which the disclosing party considers to be confidential or proprietary. Confidential Information specifically excludes trade secrets.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract creates a contradiction since trade secrets are generally considered confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts trade secret laws, which protect confidential business information, including trade secrets. By excluding trade secrets from the definition of Confidential Information, the agreement fails to protect information that is legally protectable as a trade secret, undermining the purpose of confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The modified contract explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This directly contradicts the law, as 'trade secrets' are a fundamental type of confidential business information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1839, defines 'trade secret' as encompassing various forms of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information. By excluding trade secrets, the contract fails to protect information that is legally protectable, creating a significant legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not usable. The contract contradicts the law.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or nonpermitted use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures will include the highest degree of care that either party utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will take commercially reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or nonpermitted use of Confidential Information of the other party in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information, which measures will include the degree of care that a reasonable person utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all reasonable measures' to 'commercially reasonable measures' and 'highest degree of care' to 'degree of care that a reasonable person utilizes' lowers the standard of care required, which could conflict with legal expectations for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the standard of care required to protect confidential information. The Economic Espionage Act implies a high standard of care for protecting trade secrets to maintain their legal protection. By adopting a 'commercially reasonable' and 'reasonable person' standard, the contract arguably dilutes the necessary level of protection, potentially failing to meet the threshold required to maintain trade secret status under the law.
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'all reasonable measures' and 'highest degree of care' to 'commercially reasonable measures' and 'degree of care that a reasonable person utilizes' lowers the required standard of protection for confidential information. According to the Law Explanation, this could potentially fail to meet the threshold required to maintain trade secret status under the law. The scraped snippet from the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 supports the need for maintaining trade secrets. The changed text weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, potentially undermining their legal status and thus creating a legal contradiction under misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the receiving party or any of its Representatives is requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party, the receiving party will, if legally permissible, give prompt written notice to the disclosing party so that the disclosing party may seek, at its expense, a protective order or other appropriate relief.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the receiving party or any of its Representatives is requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information of the disclosing party, the receiving party will, at its discretion, give prompt written notice to the disclosing party so that the disclosing party may seek, at its expense, a protective order or other appropriate relief.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'if legally permissible' to 'at its discretion' weakens the obligation to notify the disclosing party of a potential disclosure, potentially violating legal standards for protecting trade secrets during litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces discretion where legal standards often require notification and cooperation in protecting confidential information during legal proceedings. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c) provides a framework for protective orders, implying a good faith effort to protect confidential information. Granting the receiving party discretion undermines this framework and could be seen as a failure to reasonably protect trade secrets or other confidential information subject to discovery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates notification 'if legally permissible,' whereas the changed contract replaces this with 'at its discretion.' This introduces a contradiction because it weakens the obligation to protect confidential information during legal proceedings. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight that legal standards often require notification and cooperation in protecting confidential information. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c), as mentioned in the Law Explanation, provides a framework for protective orders. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally discusses required disclosures under Rule 26, the essence of Rule 26, as described in the Law Explanation, implies a good faith effort to protect confidential information. Replacing 'if legally permissible' with 'at its discretion' gives the receiving party leeway to decide whether to inform the disclosing party, undermining the implied duty to protect confidential information during discovery and potentially violating legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the State of California without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of Delaware without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles, except for matters pertaining to real estate, which will be governed by the laws of the state where the real estate is located.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies California law. Changing it to Delaware law while adding an exception for real estate (without any clear real estate matter) introduces confusion and potential conflict if the dispute involves trade secrets or confidential information and the real estate clause is irrelevant.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces a conflict of laws question, especially regarding the application of trade secret law. Because one company is in Delaware while the agreement mentions California, the choice of law provision now creates uncertainty about whether CUTSA applies or Delaware law applies. The vague exception for real estate matters exacerbates this issue, as it's unclear when that exception would be triggered, and the conflict of law could change how intellectual property rights are enforced.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential conflict of laws, particularly concerning trade secrets. The original text specified California law. The changed text switches to Delaware law but adds an exception for real estate matters. The Law Explanation highlights that this creates uncertainty about whether CUTSA (California Uniform Trade Secrets Act) applies. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), codified as California Civil Code Section 3426. The change introduces ambiguity on whether CUTSA applies, particularly if the dispute involves trade secrets and the real estate exception is irrelevant. This makes the contract potentially unenforceable regarding trade secrets, as the choice of law is unclear, thus creating a contradiction.
